1:
The dedication opens with a paradox the witnesses are quick to seize — that the God who has just filled the house with a cloud is the God who "said that he would dwell in the thick darkness." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fixes the word: the "thick darkness" is araphel, explained as caligo nubium, the "gloom of clouds," the same term used of Sinai in Exod. 20:21 and Deut. 4:11 and of God's canopy in Ps. 18:9; the Greek renders it orphne. He notes too that a Targum on the parallel at 1 Kings 8:12 reads "Jerusalem" in place of the darkness, but judges this probably a gloss.

The wesleyan-arminian voices dwell on the cloud and the confession. Adam Clarke observes that Solomon, seeing the cloud descend and fill the house, immediately took for granted that the LORD had accepted the place and was now present — exactly what had occurred when Moses reared the tabernacle and the glory filled it so that he could not enter, at Exod. 40:34-35. He gives the Chaldee paraphrase, that God had been pleased "to place his majesty in the city of Jerusalem, in the house of the sanctuary... to the name of his Word," and had set a dark cloud before him. John Wesley draws the devotional edge: God "has indeed made darkness his pavilion," but let this house be the residence of that darkness; for it is in the upper world that he dwells in light such as no eye can approach.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the verse into a study of motive, insisting that in all religious action it matters supremely that the eye be single. Had Solomon built the temple in the pride of his heart, as Ahasuerus made his feast merely to show the riches of his kingdom, it would not have turned to his account; instead he declares inducements that magnify the work. He built it for the glory of God as his highest end; in compliance with God's choice of Jerusalem to record his name, at v.6; in pursuance of his father's good intention that God himself had approved, at vv.7-8; and in performance of the word that his son should build the house, at vv.9-10. Temple-work, Henry adds, is often thus done — one sows and another reaps, as in John 4:37-38 — one age beginning what the next brings to perfection. The witnesses do not divide: Ellicott secures the ancient word for the gloom, Clarke and Wesley read the cloud as the token of a present God, and Henry reads the whole undertaking as a work begun with a single eye to the honour of the God who veils himself in light.
2:
Solomon answers the God who dwells in darkness with the boldness of a builder — "But I have built an house of habitation for thee" — and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears in the very grammar the note of glad response. The emphatic Hebrew, he says, is better felt as "And I, on my part, have built," where the parallel at 1 Kings 8:13 doubles the verb, banoh banithi, as if to say "I have surely built" — building as the fitting answer to what God himself had indicated. He judges this emphatic form original, kept in the Syriac though not in the Greek and Latin.

Ellicott lingers over the poetry of the words. "Habitation" translates zebul, a poetic term that occurs only five times in all the Scriptures, one of them the great theophany of Hab. 3:11 where sun and moon stand still in their "habitation"; the choice of so rare and lofty a word lifts the sentence out of plain prose into something like a hymn. "A place for thy dwelling" he traces to the Song of the Sea at Exod. 15:17, where Israel sings of the sanctuary the LORD's hands have established — another poetic expression, deliberately echoed. And the closing "for ever" is, more exactly, "through ages," a phrase found only here and in Ps. 61:5. He notes one small blemish: the connecting "and" before "a place," added in this account, slightly weakens the force of the poetic parallelism that the older text had kept cleaner.

The single witness does not divide against himself, and the weight of his reading falls on the meaning the diction carries. The verse is not a plain builder's boast but a psalm in miniature: the king who has just confessed that God dwells in unapproachable darkness now dares to name the house his own hands have raised a "habitation," a "dwelling place," a home for God "through ages." The tension the chapter will hold throughout is already set here in the choice of words — the God whom heaven and the heaven of heavens cannot contain has been given, and has consented to receive, a fixed place among men. Ellicott's care for a rare noun and an echoed phrase opens, almost without comment, upon the whole mystery the prayer will press: that the eternal One should have an "everlasting" house of clay and cedar, built by a mortal king, and yet call it his rest. The philology guards the letter; the letter, so guarded, testifies to the wonder that the God of the thick darkness has a home in the world he made.
13:
Only the chronicler tells us of the platform on which the king prayed — "Solomon had made a brasen scaffold, of five cubits long, and five cubits broad, and three cubits high, and had set it in the midst of the court," and there he kneeled and spread forth his hands toward heaven. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes at once that this verse has no counterpart in the older account, though it may once have followed 1 Kings 8:22; at least, he says, the marks of the chronicler's own individual style are not apparent in it, so that it may well be an authentic notice preserved here alone.

He fixes the strange word for the platform. "Scaffold" is literally a "pan" — kiyor, the same term used for the lavers at 2 Chronicles 4:6 — so that the structure "looked like a laver turned upside down," a great basin of bronze inverted and doubtless hollow underneath, on which the king could stand raised above the crowd. He compares the analogous platform on which the Levites stood at Nehemiah 9:4. The attitude Ellicott illustrates from antiquity: to kneel upon the knees and spread forth the hands is a posture of prayer that may be seen figured upon the monuments of ancient Egypt. And he notes that for "toward heaven" the chronicler has used the exact and fuller Hebrew form where the older text had the less precise one — a small refinement of the letter.

The single witness leaves the picture standing in its own dignity. Solomon does not pray from the throne, nor from within the sanctuary where only the priests might go, but from a bronze platform set "in the midst of the court," in full view of the whole congregation — lifted up so that all may see, yet kneeling so that all may see him humbled. The very height that exalts him he uses to abase himself; raised above the people, he goes down upon his knees before God. There is a lesson in the geometry of it that the chronicler, alone preserving the detail, plainly means the reader to feel: the greatest king in Israel, at the height of his glory and on the day of his greatest work, takes the posture of a suppliant, hands open and empty toward heaven, on a stage built precisely so that a nation might watch its sovereign kneel. Ellicott's patient recovery of the "pan" turned upside down, and of the Egyptian parallel to the outspread hands, restores to us the physical truth of the scene — a scaffold of bronze, a kneeling king, a court full of standing people — and lets the plain arrangement of bodies preach its own sermon on humility before the God who dwells in the darkness above.
12:
Solomon takes his stand to pray — "he stood before the altar of the LORD in the presence of all the congregation of Israel, and spread forth his hands" — and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry treats the moment as the true consecration of the house. By this prayer, he says, the temple was made a figure of Christ, the great Mediator, through whom we are to offer all our prayers, expect all God's favours, and to whom we must have an eye in everything wherein we have to do with God. Henry reads the long petition that follows as a treasury of doctrine and of pattern. In it he finds laid down that the God of Israel is a being of incomparable perfection, with none like him in heaven or earth, at v.14; that he is infinite and immense, whom the heaven of heavens cannot contain, at v.18; that he alone knows the hearts of the children of men, at v.30; and that there is no such thing as sinless perfection in this life, at v.36, agreeable to his own later word in Eccl. 7:20.

Henry marks too the wisdom of the prayer's order. Solomon supposes that troublesome times will come, that affliction will drive to God those who had said to him "Depart," and that even strangers will come from afar to worship — and he frames his petitions accordingly, that God would own this house and have an eye to it as the place of his name, at v.20; that he would hear and forgive, at v.21; that he would judge according to equity, return in mercy to the penitent, welcome the stranger, and always plead his people's cause. "The prayer that will speed," Henry insists, "must be warranted by the word" — Solomon asks nothing but what God had first said he would do.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends, as ever, to the posture itself. "Stood," he cautions, means only that the king "took his place"; it is not implied that he remained standing, for the next verse will show him kneeling — the same idiom of taking one's stand that appears at 1 Samuel 17:51 and at v.3 above. "Spread forth his hands," he notes, is toward heaven, as the parallel makes explicit and the Syriac and Arabic supply. The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott fixes the bodily attitude — the king taking his place before the altar, hands lifted to heaven — and Henry unfolds the soul of the act, reading in this spread of the hands the whole shape of gospel prayer: a mediator standing between God and the people, pleading the promises of God back to God, and making the house a figure of the one Mediator through whom every later prayer must pass. The verse is thus the doorway of the great prayer, and the witnesses open it from both sides at once.
14:
The prayer proper opens with adoration — "O LORD God of Israel, there is no God like thee in the heaven, nor in the earth; which keepest covenant, and shewest mercy unto thy servants that walk before thee with all their hearts." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the incomparable perfection of God at the head of the whole prayer: we cannot describe him, but this we know, there is none like him; all the creatures have their fellow-creatures, but the Creator has not his peer, being infinitely above all and over all. And those who set God always before them and walk before him with all their hearts, Henry adds, shall find him both faithful and kind, as good as his word and better, keeping covenant with them and showing them mercy.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that "in the heaven nor in the earth" is abridged from the older "in the heaven above, and upon the earth beneath" at 1 Kings 8:23, the rest of the verse standing word for word. He hears in the Syriac's expansion a curious echo of the Lord's Prayer — "Thy will is done on earth beneath" — and observes that the Assyrians too spoke of their gods as "without an equal," a rival they had not, so that Solomon's confession takes up and out-tops the common language of the nations. The phrase "keepest covenant and shewest mercy," he explains, is more exactly "keeping the covenant and the mercy" — that is, the covenanted mercy, the two bound into one, as at Isaiah 55:3.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses fill out the confession from two sides. Adam Clarke glosses "that walk before thee with all their hearts" from the Targum — "with all the will of their souls and with all the affection of their hearts" — pressing the wholeness the phrase demands. John Wesley draws the christological frame that will govern the reading of the whole prayer: by this prayer the temple of Solomon is made a figure of Christ, the great Mediator, through whom we offer all our prayers and expect all God's favours. The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott secures the abridged wording and the covenanted-mercy of the Hebrew; Clarke deepens the "with all their hearts"; and Henry and Wesley together lift the verse toward its double climax — that the God who has no equal in heaven or earth is yet the God who binds himself in covenant, and who has bound himself, in the end, in Christ. The adoration is not cold; the incomparable God is the covenant-keeping, mercy-showing God, and it is on that union of majesty and faithfulness that every petition to follow will lean.
18:
At the height of his confidence Solomon is seized by wonder — "But will God in very deed dwell with men on the earth? behold, heaven and the heaven of heavens cannot contain thee; how much less this house which I have built!" The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the verse as the prayer's great confession of the divine immensity: God is a being infinite and immense, whom the heaven, and heaven of heavens, cannot contain, and to whose felicity nothing is added by the utmost we can do in his service; he is infinitely beyond the bounds of the creation and infinitely above the praises of all intelligent creatures. That such a God should stoop to dwell with men is the marvel the whole temple is built around.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke enlarges the wonder through the Targum's paraphrase: "who could have imagined, who could have thought it credible, that God should place his majesty among men dwelling upon earth? Behold, the highest heavens, the middle heavens, and the lowest heavens cannot bear the glory of thy majesty... nor can this house which I have built contain Thee." The threefold heavens only deepen the disproportion — if the very skies cannot hold him, how shall a house of stone?

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the opening particle. The Hebrew is almost an exclamation — "what? will God in very deed dwell?" — the incredulous idiom that the Greek imitates and the Vulgate renders "is it then credible that God should dwell?" And he marks a phrase peculiar to the chronicler: "with men" is not in the older account at 1 Kings 8:27, where the Syriac reads instead "with his people Israel." That added "with men" widens the wonder from Israel to the race, and Ellicott points forward to its answer in Revelation 21:3, where the voice from the throne declares that the tabernacle of God is with men, and he will dwell with them. The witnesses do not divide. Henry and Clarke press the immensity that makes the condescension incredible; Ellicott fixes the incredulous grammar and the chronicler's own "with men" that turns the particular temple into a sign of the universal dwelling to come. The verse holds the two truths in one breath — that God cannot be contained, and that God will condescend — and Solomon, at the summit of his achievement, bows before the mystery that the God whom the heaven of heavens cannot hold has chosen, and will yet more fully choose, to dwell with men.
22:
Solomon begins the great series of cases with the courtroom — "If a man sin against his neighbour, and an oath be laid upon him to make him swear, and the oath come before thine altar in this house." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke treats the verse as the first of the seven cases the king lays before God, referring the reader for their full handling to the older account at 1 Kings 8:31-46. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott works the grammar of the scene closely. "If a man sin" replaces the older "whatsoever a man sin"; "an oath be laid upon him" means that his neighbour, or people generally, "lay an oath upon him," as in the law of the deposit at Exodus 22:11; and "the oath come before thine altar" is more exactly that the accused man "enter upon an oath before thine altar," a solemn swearing at the sanctuary, comparable to the oath of allegiance in Ezekiel 17:13. The versions, he notes, read "and he come and swear before thine altar," a difference of a single prefixed letter.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws out why such a case should stand first. He supposes that when doubts and controversies arise between man and man, both sides will agree to appeal to God, laying an oath upon the person whose testimony must decide the matter. The religious reverence of an oath, Henry observes, is ancient, and it may be presumed will continue as long as there remain any relics of conscience and right reason among men. Where human evidence fails, the oath refers the hidden truth to the God who knows it, and asks him to judge between the parties.

The witnesses do not divide. Clarke frames the verse as the opening of the seven petitions; Ellicott fixes the legal act — the accused entering upon an oath at the very altar; and Henry reads the spiritual meaning of the institution, that men in their disputes fall back at last upon the God who sees the heart. The scene is striking: a private quarrel between neighbours brought, by way of a sworn oath, into the courts of the temple, so that the God who dwells there is made the judge of a matter no earthly court could settle. Solomon asks that God would honour that appeal — hearing from heaven, requiting the false swearer and clearing the true, as the next verse will say. The oath before the altar is thus the meeting-place of law and worship: the sanctuary becomes a tribunal, and the God of the covenant is invoked as the searcher of hearts and the vindicator of the wronged. Henry's confidence that the reverence of an oath will outlast every age rests on the same ground the verse assumes — that conscience knows itself answerable to a God who cannot be deceived.
36:
The last and darkest case opens with a confession that shadows the whole human race — "if they sin against thee (for there is no man which sinneth not), and thou be angry with them, and deliver them over before their enemies, and they carry them away captives unto a land far off or near." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fastens on the parenthesis, "there is no man which sinneth not," referring the reader for its full consideration to the older account at 1 Kings 8:46; the words state a universal truth that no descendant of Adam can evade. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the wording of the calamity: "their enemies" is more exactly "an enemy," and the clause "they carry them away" describes the deportation into captivity, near or far, that the following verses will make the setting of the final plea.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry seizes the parenthesis as one of the prayer's cardinal doctrines — that there is no such thing as a sinless perfection to be found in this life. "There is no man who sinneth not," Solomon says; nay, Henry adds, no man who doeth good and sinneth not, agreeable to the king's own later verdict in Ecclesiastes 7:20, "there is not a just man upon earth, that doeth good, and sinneth not." The supposition of a captivity for sin rests upon the certainty that the people will sin, for sin is the universal condition of men.

The witnesses do not divide, and there is a notable convergence in it: the parenthesis that flatly denies any sinless perfection is affirmed as heartily by the wesleyan Clarke as by the puritan Henry, both taking Solomon's words as a plain statement that all men sin. Ellicott fixes the outward shape of the judgment — an angry God delivering his people to an enemy who carries them into exile — while Clarke and Henry press the inward truth that makes such a judgment always possible: that no nation, and no man, can claim the innocence that would put it beyond the reach of chastening. The case is the bleakest Solomon frames, for it foresees not defeat or drought but the total catastrophe of captivity in a far land; yet even here the ground of hope is already laid in the honesty of the parenthesis. Precisely because "there is no man which sinneth not," the door of repentance the next verses open must be a door for all; a people who could not claim to be sinless can always claim the mercy promised to the penitent. The verse thus prepares for the prayer's great climax by first stripping away every pretension to innocence, so that when the exiles turn and confess, they turn as what they are — sinners, like all men, thrown upon the mercy of God.
37:
Into the darkness of exile the prayer plants the possibility of return — "yet if they bethink themselves in the land whither they are carried captive, and turn and pray... saying, We have sinned, we have done amiss, and have dealt wickedly." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke glosses "if they bethink themselves" from the Targum — "if thy fear should return into their hearts" — making the first motion of repentance the revival of a lost reverence; and he refers the whole of this closing plea to the full treatment in the older account at 1 Kings 8:22-53. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders "bethink themselves" more exactly "if they take it to heart," that is, repent, comparing the call to lay it to heart in Deuteronomy 4:39.

Ellicott's finest observation falls on the threefold confession. "We have sinned, we have done amiss, and have dealt wickedly" employs the same three verbs that recur in the great penitential prayers of Psalms 106:6 and Daniel 9:5 — and, he shows, they mount in a deliberate climax: "we have slipped," or missed the mark; "we have done crookedly"; "we have been godless." The confession does not merely repeat itself but deepens, moving from the failure that falls short, through the perversity that bends the wrong way, to the ungodliness that is the root of both.

The witnesses do not divide. Clarke traces the inward beginning of repentance to the returning fear of God, and Ellicott traces its outward form in the ascending honesty of its confession. Between them the verse maps the anatomy of true penitence: it begins when a people "take it to heart" — when, in the far land of their captivity, the fear of God stirs again in hearts that had forgotten him — and it comes to speech in a confession that will not spare itself, naming the sin as slipping, as crookedness, and at last as sheer godlessness. There is no excuse in it, no shifting of blame; the exiles own the whole of their guilt in its rising degrees. And it is just this unsparing self-accusation that the prayer counts on, for the mercy Solomon is about to invoke is mercy for the penitent, and the penitent must first say without reserve what he is. Clarke's "if thy fear should return" and Ellicott's climbing climax together show repentance as both a gift and an act — the fear of God returning by grace, and the guilty heart answering with a confession that leaves nothing hidden. It is the turning-point of the whole final case, the moment in the far country when a captive people, taking it to heart, begin the road home by telling God the truth about themselves.
41:
The chronicler closes the prayer with words drawn from a psalm — "Now therefore arise, O LORD God, into thy resting place, thou, and the ark of thy strength: let thy priests, O LORD God, be clothed with salvation, and let thy saints rejoice in goodness." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott traces the whole to Psalms 132:8-9, and marks the chronicler's fingerprints upon it. "Now therefore" is his own addition; the rare title "O LORD God," Iahweh Elohim, which the psalm does not use, occurs three times in these two verses and nowhere else in the prayer, though the chronicler is fond of it elsewhere and it never appears in the older books of Kings. "Into thy resting place" employs a late word, nuah, where the psalm has the common menuhah. And here Ellicott registers a real reservation: "the idea that the sanctuary is God's resting-place," he judges, "is not in keeping with the spirit of the prayer," which has throughout begged God to "hear from heaven thy dwelling place," and had confessed at v.18 that no house can contain him.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses take the "resting place" in exactly the opposite sense, as the very heart of the petition. John Wesley paraphrases the "arise": "O thou that sittest in the heavens, arise from the throne of thy glory, and come down into this place, which thou hast appointed for thy constant habitation, from which thou wilt not remove, as formerly thou hast done, from place to place." "The ark of thy strength," he adds, is the sign and instrument of God's great power put forth for his people, and "salvation" the protection and benediction with which the priests are to be encompassed. Adam Clarke reads "let thy saints rejoice in goodness" with Jarchi of the abundance of tithes and goods given the Levites for keeping the ark and singing before it.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry stands with the wesleyans on the substance: Solomon prays that God would take possession of the temple and keep possession, making it his resting-place — "Thou and the ark; for what will the ark do without the God of the ark?" — that he would clothe the ministers with salvation, making them not only saved but instrumental to save others, and that the service might turn to the joy of all the Lord's people, that his saints might rejoice in goodness, as in Psalms 65:4. So a genuine difference stands: whether the temple truly becomes God's "resting place," his settled and abiding habitation as Wesley and Henry devoutly read it, or whether that psalm-borrowed phrase sits a little uneasily with a prayer that has kept heaven for God's dwelling and the house only for his regard, as Ellicott cautions. The one reads the words as the prayer's warm climax, God consenting to rest among his people; the other hears in them a note carried over from the psalm that the prayer's own theology would qualify. Both agree on what is asked for the priests and the saints — salvation and gladness — and both leave the verse where the psalm leaves it, with the ark of God's strength carried up into the house and the whole ministry clothed and rejoicing before him.
42:
The prayer ends on a plea that reaches beyond Solomon to David and beyond David to the Christ — "O LORD God, turn not away the face of thine anointed: remember the mercies of David thy servant." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott shows how the chronicler has reshaped his source. The couplet comes from Psalms 132:10, "For thy servant David's sake turn not away the face of thine anointed," but its members are transposed, and the language of the first is modified to bring in the phrase "the mercies of David" — that is, the mercies God promised to David, the "sure mercies" of Isaiah 55:3 and Psalms 89:49. The opening "O LORD God" is not in the psalm; the temple-invocation is used, Ellicott notes, as in the priestly blessing of Numbers 6:24-26. "Turn not away the face" means "deny not the request" of the anointed, as at 1 Kings 2:16; and the imperative "remember," zokrah, is a rare form found besides only in Nehemiah.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke gives the plea its humble edge from Jarchi — "at least do me good; and if not for my sake, do it for thy own sake" — and observes that these two closing verses have no place in the older account at 1 Kings 8:22-53, being the chronicler's own addition to the prayer. The suppliant asks not on the ground of his own worth but on God's.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears the two grounds on which Solomon rests his whole plea — his own relation to God and God's covenant with his father. "Turn not away the face of thine anointed": Lord, thou hast appointed me king, and wilt thou not own me? And "remember the mercies of David thy servant" — the promises of the everlasting covenant that were David's great support. But Henry will not let the verse rest in David; he reads it, with the prayer's whole christological frame, as fulfilled in a greater Anointed. We come, he says, in the name of the Lord Jesus, God's Anointed, his Messiah, his Christ — "so the LXX" renders the word — whom the Father hears always and will never turn away, pleading no righteousness of our own but the "sure mercies of David" secured to us in him, as Acts 13:34 applies the promise. The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott traces the chronicler's careful transposition of the psalm and his forging of the phrase "the mercies of David"; Clarke marks the humility of the appeal and its place as the prayer's own added close; and Henry lifts the "anointed" from Solomon through David to the Christ, in whom the face of the true Anointed is never turned away and the mercies of David are made sure for ever. So the great dedication ends not with Solomon's merit but with God's covenant — the mercies promised to David, and, to the eye of faith, fulfilled in David's greater Son.
3:
Between the vision of the darkness-dwelling God and the long prayer that follows comes a hinge of pure action — "the king turned his face, and blessed the whole congregation of Israel: and all the congregation of Israel stood." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes first that the verse stands word for word as in the older narrative at 1 Kings 8:14, the chronicler copying his source without change — a small but telling witness to the fidelity with which he hands on the record. "Turned," he explains, is more exactly "turned round," the same verb used at 1 Chronicles 10:14; and "stood" is better rendered "was standing," a description of the assembly's posture rather than a fresh action.

The plain motion the words describe repays a moment's attention. Solomon has been facing the house, addressing the God who has filled it with cloud; now he turns his back on the sanctuary and his face to the people, and before he pours out his petition to heaven he first pronounces a blessing upon the congregation. The order is not accidental. The king who will presently kneel as a suppliant stands first as a mediator between God and the nation, and the whole assembly — turned out in its thousands for the dedication — is on its feet to receive both the blessing and the prayer. The chronicler, who loves to gather the leaders and the people into these great scenes of common worship, fixes the picture: a standing nation, a king turned to face it, a benediction spoken before ever the supplication begins.

The single witness does not divide, and Ellicott's grammatical care leaves the verse exactly where the text sets it. What the "turned round" and the "was standing" establish is the choreography of a public act of devotion — nothing hidden, nothing private, the king's face and the people's posture alike open to view. The verse is a seam, joining the descent of the glory to the ascent of the prayer, and the chronicler will not let it pass without recording that between the two the whole congregation of Israel stood, and stood to be blessed. It is the same instinct that runs through his account of Solomon's reign from the first: that a kingdom rightly ordered brings its king and its people together, on their feet before God, so that the blessing spoken over them and the prayer offered for them fall upon a nation gathered as one. The bare notice, faithfully copied, thus carries its own quiet weight in the drama of the dedication — the moment when the mediating king turns from God to men to bless, before turning back from men to God to plead.
4:
The blessing becomes a doxology, and its theme is the perfect correspondence of God's word and God's work — "who hath with his hands fulfilled that which he spake with his mouth to my father David." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott draws out the exact shape of the Hebrew: God "spake by his mouth with David my father, and by his hands fulfilled" — the mouth that promised and the hands that performed set deliberately over against each other, so that the sentence is built on the balance of speaking and doing. He points the reader back to the promises themselves, the covenant word given through Nathan and the anointing at Hebron recorded in 1 Chronicles 11:2 and the great dynastic oracle of 1 Chronicles 17:4-14.

The one textual nicety Ellicott marks is small and characteristic: where the older account at 1 Kings 8:15 speaks of God's "hand," the chronicler writes "hands," in the plural — and the unpointed Hebrew of Kings, he observes, might in fact be read either way, so that the difference is one of vowels only, not of substance, a point he ties to the same variation at v.15 below. It is exactly the kind of trace his method looks for: a later writer copying an older text and settling, by a hair, one of the two readings the consonants allowed.

Yet the theology the grammar guards is the heart of the verse, and it is the theme on which the whole prayer will turn. Solomon does not bless God for a favour freely conferred but for a promise faithfully kept: the mouth spoke, the hands fulfilled, and between the speaking and the doing not one word fell to the ground. The finished temple standing behind him is the visible proof of it — what God pledged to David he has now, through David's son, brought to pass with his own hands. Ellicott's insistence on the mouth-and-hands parallel is not idle philology; it exposes the very ground on which Solomon will presently dare to pray, for a God who has kept the first half of his covenant to David may be pressed, in faith, to keep the rest. The doxology of this verse is thus the platform of the petition that follows: the king rehearses a promise already performed precisely so that he may plead the promises not yet performed, confident that the hands which built the house will not fail the word that spoke it. Praise, in Solomon's mouth, is the memory of promises kept made into the argument for promises to keep.
5:
God's remembered word to David, quoted in the prayer, reaches back to the Exodus — "Since the day that I brought forth my people out of the land of Egypt I chose no city... neither chose I any man to be a ruler over my people Israel." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the chronicler writes "my people out of the land of Egypt" where the older account has "my people Israel out of Egypt," a small variation he compares with 2 Chronicles 5:10. His larger interest falls on the second half of the verse, the clause about choosing no ruler — the Hebrew nagid, a "leader" — which is not found in the parallel at all, for there the corresponding words form the last clause of 1 Kings 8:16. Ellicott finds nothing in the language against the supposition that these words originally belonged to the older text and dropped out of it, so that the chronicler may here preserve what Kings has lost.

The clause "neither chose I any man to be a ruler" draws from him a pointed theological observation. Saul, he reminds the reader, was originally the people's choice and not God's — demanded by Israel when they said "make us a king to judge us like all the nations," at 1 Samuel 8:5 — so that the true and abiding election of a ruler waited for David. Holy Scripture, Ellicott insists, nowhere teaches that the voice of the people is the voice of God; the popular clamour that raised Saul is not to be confused with the divine decree that set David and his line upon the throne. The verse quietly distinguishes the two.

The single witness carries the verse, and the point it leaves standing is the sovereign freedom of God's electing choice. From the Exodus onward, the LORD had bound himself to no city and no dynasty until, in his own time, he named both — Jerusalem for his house and David for his throne, as the next verse will declare. The long silence of the intervening centuries, in which no place was chosen for the name and no man confirmed as ruler, only sets off the deliberateness of the double election when at last it came. What Israel had grasped at prematurely in demanding Saul, God granted in his own way in giving David; and Solomon, pleading this history in prayer, rests his whole petition on the fact that the city he has built in and the dynasty he springs from are not the people's arrangement but God's appointment. Ellicott's care to recover the lost clause and to mark the lesson of Saul thus serves the prayer's deepest logic — that the temple stands where it stands, and the king kneels where he kneels, by the choice of God alone.
6:
The long silence breaks in a double election — "But I have chosen Jerusalem, that my name might be there; and have chosen David to be over my people Israel." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott's note is brief, marking only that the opening particle is better felt as "and I have chosen Jerusalem," and that some manuscripts omit the verse altogether — a slip of the copyist's eye, most likely, from the repeated "chosen," rather than any doubt about the sense. The words themselves are the pivot on which the whole recital turns.

For here the two great choices of Israel's God are set side by side, place and person, city and king. Against the background of v.5, where from the Exodus onward the LORD had chosen no city and confirmed no ruler, the emphatic "but" now announces that the waiting is over: Jerusalem is chosen for the name, and David is chosen for the throne. The one election grounds the temple, the other grounds the dynasty, and the two are inseparable — the house Solomon has built stands in the chosen city, and the king who built it sits by the chosen line. The name of God is to dwell in a particular place because the God of Israel is not a god of vague and universal shrines but the covenant LORD who binds himself to a definite spot and a definite family.

The single witness does not elaborate, and the verse needs little elaboration, for its force lies in the sheer fact of the divine "I have chosen." Everything in Solomon's prayer rests upon it. He can ask God to keep his eyes open upon this house, and no other, because God has chosen this city for his name; he can plead the covenant with David, and no other, because God has chosen David to be over his people. The choice is free, unearned, and irreversible — Jerusalem did not commend itself, nor David his house, by any merit that compelled the election. It is the same sovereign grace that will run through every petition to come, and that the whole later Scripture will gather up when it speaks of a chosen people, a chosen stone, a chosen Son. Ellicott's spare comment leaves the weight where the text puts it: on the twice-spoken "chosen," the bedrock of the temple and the throne alike, and the warrant for all that Solomon is about to ask of the God who chose both the city and the king.
9:
The word to David carried both a refusal and a promise — "thou shalt not build the house; but thy son... he shall build the house for my name" — and the witnesses attend to the sharp turn between them. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott brings out the emphasis the Hebrew lays on the pronoun: not merely "thou shalt not build," but "only thou, thou shalt not build," the stress falling on David himself, as if to mark the personal exclusion. The next clause he reads more exactly as "for thy son that shall come forth" — the son who is to build — noting that the older account and some manuscripts have "but thy son," a difference of a single preposition, the rest of the verse standing as in 1 Kings 8:19.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the same turn for its lesson in the long patience of God's work. "Thus one sows, and another reaps," he says; the design was David's, the execution Solomon's, and the two are not rivals but partners across a generation. And he draws the moral wide: let not the wisest of men think it any disparagement to pursue the good designs which those that went before them had laid. The father who was forbidden to build was not therefore dishonoured, nor the son who built the less obedient for building on another's foundation; the honour of the work is shared between the heart that conceived it and the hands that finished it.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott secures the grammar of the exclusion and the succession — the emphatic "thou" who may not build, the "son" who shall — while Wesley draws out the providence that binds the two. Between them the verse becomes a small parable of how God accomplishes his purposes in the world: rarely in a single life, often across the joints of the generations, one age beginning what the next completes. David's exclusion was no punishment but an appointment; he was permitted to prepare, forbidden to perform, and the performance reserved for the son whom God had named beforehand. That reservation is itself a mercy, for it made the temple not one man's monument but the fruit of a promise kept over time, the LORD faithfully carrying his word from the father's heart into the son's hands. Solomon, rehearsing this in prayer, stands as the living proof that the God who spoke to David has done exactly what he said — refused the one, appointed the other, and brought the house to pass in its appointed hour. Wesley's "one sows, another reaps" catches the whole of it, and turns a family's history into a rule for every servant of God who must lay foundations that others will build upon.
10:
The recital of promise now closes on performance — "The LORD therefore hath performed his word that he hath spoken: for I am risen up in the room of David my father, and set on the throne of Israel." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens each verb. "Hath performed" is more exactly "hath established," or "ratified," his word — literally "caused to rise up," as though the promise itself, long lying as a spoken thing, had now been made to stand and take substance. "Spoken," he adds, means "promised," the word carrying its covenantal force; and "set" is better "seated," the king not merely placed but enthroned. The verse, he notes, has no real variant from the older account at 1 Kings 8:20, the chronicler here copying faithfully.

The single note opens on the theme that has governed the whole passage. Ellicott's rendering — the LORD has "caused his word to rise up" — catches the exact correspondence the prayer keeps pressing: the promise spoken to David has literally risen and stood in the person of Solomon, seated on the throne, and in the house behind him. There is a deliberate play in it, for the same verb that lifts a fallen thing to its feet is used of God's establishing his word; the promise that might have seemed a mere saying has been raised into a reality one can see and touch.

And so the verse gathers the doxology of v.4 and the recital of vv.5-9 into a single confession of fulfilment. Solomon names two proofs that God has kept his word: he himself is "risen up in the room of David," the son promised to succeed the father; and he is "seated on the throne of Israel," the dynasty promised to endure. Both were spoken to David before ever they came to pass; both now stand accomplished. The king does not claim the throne as his own achievement but receives it as the establishing of a divine word — he sits where he sits because God said he would. Ellicott's care to render the verb as "established" rather than the vaguer "performed" keeps the emphasis exactly where the chronicler wants it: not on Solomon's accession as a political event but on the faithfulness of God, whose spoken promise has been made to rise up and stand in a living king and a finished temple. It is the last stone in the platform of fulfilment from which, in the verses to come, the prayer of petition will be launched — a prayer that can plead promises unkept only because it stands upon a promise so plainly kept.
11:
The recital ends where the temple's whole meaning centres — "in it have I put the ark, wherein is the covenant of the LORD." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the clause as a deliberate abridgment: the chronicler writes simply "I have set there the ark," where the older account at 1 Kings 8:21 has the fuller "I have set there a place for the ark," and the Syriac and Arabic keep something of that fuller form, "I have prepared a place for the ark." The shortening is characteristic — the chronicler clips the phrase without loss of sense, since to put the ark is to give it its place.

On the second clause Ellicott is precise: "wherein is the covenant of the LORD" means that within the ark lie the two tables of the Law, the tables of stone on which the Ten Words were written, as he had explained at 2 Chronicles 5:10. The "covenant" is not an abstraction but a document — the very terms of the bond between God and Israel, laid up in the golden chest at the heart of the house.

The single witness leaves the theology to declare itself, and it is considerable. Everything the prayer has rehearsed — the choice of Jerusalem, the choice of David, the word spoken and performed — comes to rest on this: that the finished temple is built around the ark, and the ark holds the covenant. The house is not a shrine for an image, as the temples of the nations were, but a house for the covenant of the living God; its inmost treasure is the written pledge of his faithfulness and the written claim of his law. Solomon names it last in his recital because it is first in importance. The God who dwells in the thick darkness has consented to dwell here precisely because here, in the ark, lie the tables that bind him to Israel and Israel to him. When in the petitions to come the king pleads for mercy on a people who have broken that very law, he pleads before the ark that contains it — the covenant both accusing and, through the mercy-seat above it, sheltering the nation. Ellicott's spare identification of the "covenant" with the two tables thus fixes the pivot of the whole chapter: the temple exists to house the covenant, and the prayer that follows is offered over the covenant, appealing from the law within the ark to the mercy of the God enthroned above it.
16:
From adoration Solomon turns to the promise he means to plead — "keep with thy servant David my father that which thou hast promised him, saying, There shall not fail thee a man in my sight to sit upon the throne of Israel; yet so that thy children take heed to their way to walk in my law." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the opening "and now," the same connective that opens v.17, and reads "keep that which thou hast promised" as a plea for the further promise — Solomon having just acknowledged, at v.10, the fulfilment of the former one. The clause "there shall not fail thee a man" he traces through the Greek and the Vulgate that the Authorized Version follows, and notes that "to sit" is in Hebrew simply "sitting."

The hinge of the verse, for Ellicott, is the conditional. "Yet so that" is more exactly "only if" — a single condition sharply stated, "provided that" the sons take heed to walk in the law, the Greek and Latin alike marking the proviso. And on the last words he catches the chronicler's careful hand: where the older account at 1 Kings 8:25 reads "walk before me," the chronicler writes "walk in my law" — the only variant in the verse — avoiding, Ellicott suggests, a seeming tautology, and at the same time fixing the condition upon obedience to the written law rather than the vaguer "before me."

The single witness leaves the theology of the verse exposed in that one substituted phrase. The promise to David is dynastic and sure, yet it is not unconditional in its enjoyment: the throne will not fail of an heir, but the heirs must walk in the law if they are to sit on it. Ellicott's care to render "only if" keeps the proviso from being softened away. Here is the tension the books of Kings and Chronicles will trace to its bitter end — a covenant faithful on God's side, hung on the obedience of the sons on Israel's side, and finally broken by the very kings it was meant to establish. Solomon prays the promise in the flush of its first fulfilment, standing himself as the "man" who has not failed to sit on David's throne; but the condition he repeats over his own reign is the seed of the whole later history, and the substituted "walk in my law" points forward to the law kept in the ark beneath him as the measure by which his house will be judged. The chronicler's small change of wording thus carries the prayer's gravest note: the surest of promises still asks obedience of those who would inherit it.
17:
Solomon presses the plea to its point — "Now then, O LORD God of Israel, let thy word be verified, which thou hast spoken unto thy servant David." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears in "be verified" the very language of David's own prayer at 1 Chronicles 17:23, where the father had asked that the thing God had spoken might be "established for ever"; the son now takes up the father's word and prays it again over the finished house. He notes that the Greek and the Syriac add the softening particle "I pray" that the older account keeps, and that "thy word" is, as throughout this prayer, better felt as "thy promise," the same term rendered so at vv.10 and 15. The phrasing "unto thy servant David" is in Hebrew "to thy servant, to David," where the older text at 1 Kings 8:26 adds "my father."

The single witness draws the whole movement of the prayer to a fine edge. To ask that God's word be "verified" is not to doubt it but to lay hold of it — to bring the spoken promise back to the God who spoke it and ask him to make it good. Ellicott's cross-reference to David's prayer is the key: the same faith that moved the father moves the son, and the two prayers, a generation apart, are almost one prayer, echoing across the years the single request that God would stand by what he had said. The verb "be verified," literally "be confirmed" or "made faithful," treats the promise as something already firm in itself that asks only to be shown firm in the event.

And so this brief verse states, more nakedly than any other, the principle on which the entire dedication prayer is built. Solomon does not come to God with requests of his own devising; he comes with God's own promise, and asks only that it be honoured. The boldness of the prayer is the boldness of faith holding God to his word — a boldness that is really the deepest reverence, for it takes the divine promise so seriously that it dares to plead it. What David asked in hope, before the temple was raised, Solomon now asks in the confidence of a man standing in the very house that proves the first half of the promise kept. The word that gave him the throne and the house may surely be trusted for the rest; and to pray "let thy word be verified" is to rest the whole future of David's line, and the whole efficacy of the prayers to come, on nothing but the faithfulness of God to what he has spoken. Ellicott's linking of father and son shows the prayer to be no novelty but the family's settled way of dealing with God — by promise, and by the pleading of promise.
19:
From the vastness of God the prayer turns, almost abruptly, to the smallness of the suppliant — "Have respect therefore to the prayer of thy servant, and to his supplication, O LORD my God, to hearken unto the cry and the prayer which thy servant prayeth before thee." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the opening more exactly "but turn thou unto" — the Authorized Version following the Greek and the Vulgate — a verb of God's turning his face and attention to the one who prays. He notes only that the older account at 1 Kings 8:28 adds a "to-day" that the chronicler here lets fall.

The single witness leaves the force of the verse to its own remarkable movement, and the movement is everything. In the breath before, Solomon has confessed that the heaven of heavens cannot contain God and that no house can hold him; and now, without pause, he asks that this same illimitable God would "turn" and "hearken" to the cry of a single servant. The juxtaposition is the very heart of the prayer. The God who is too great for the universe is asked to bend his ear to one man's supplication — and the "but" with which the verse opens is the pivot on which that astonishing request swings. Ellicott's "turn thou unto" catches it precisely: the infinite One is entreated to turn, as a man turns to listen to another, toward the prayer offered before him.

Three words for the same act pile up in the verse — "prayer," "supplication," "cry" — and the repetition is not idle. It is the language of a man in earnest, heaping synonym on synonym because the one thing he wants is to be heard. And running through them all is the humble self-designation "thy servant," twice repeated: Solomon, the king who has built the house, names himself before God only as a servant who prays. The verse thus teaches the posture of all true prayer, that it approaches the greatness of God not with presumption but with the boldness of a servant who knows his master will not despise him. Precisely because God is so great, it is a wonder that he should stoop to hear; and precisely because he has promised to hear, the servant dares to ask that he would. Ellicott's spare comment, fixing the sense of "turn," leaves standing the paradox the chapter keeps pressing — the transcendent God entreated to attend, and consenting to attend, to the cry of the man kneeling on the bronze platform below.
20:
The plea grows specific — "that thine eyes may be open upon this house day and night, upon the place whereof thou hast said that thou wouldest put thy name there; to hearken unto the prayer which thy servant prayeth toward this place." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders "upon" more exactly as "unto" or "toward," and marks a small transposition: the chronicler writes "day and night," as at Psalms 1:2, where the older account at 1 Kings 8:29 has "night and day," the writer substituting the more usual phrase. "Prayeth," he adds, is "shall pray" — at any time whatever; and "toward this place" is to be taken strictly, for the temple of Jerusalem was, and is, the Kebla of the Jew, the fixed point toward which prayer is directed, as when Daniel opened his windows toward the city at Daniel 6:10, a usage the prayer itself will paraphrase at v.34.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears in the petition the first of Solomon's requests, and the principle beneath it. Solomon asks that God would own this house and have an eye to it as the place of which he had said he would put his name there; and he could not, in faith, have asked such peculiar favour for this house above any other had God not first said it should be his rest. "The prayer that will speed," Henry insists, "must be warranted by the word." We may with humble confidence pray to God to be well pleased with us in Jesus Christ, because he has declared himself well pleased in him — "This is my beloved Son"; but he says not now of any house, "This is my beloved place."

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott fixes the directedness of the prayer — the eyes of God open toward the house, the prayers of men offered toward it — and Henry grounds the whole petition in the word already spoken. Between them the verse yields both its mechanism and its warrant. The temple becomes the meeting-point of two gazes: God's eyes turned upon the place where he has set his name, and the worshipper's face turned toward the place where God has promised to look; and prayer succeeds not because the building is holy in itself but because God has bound his name to it by his own word. Henry's caution keeps the point evangelical — the house was the appointed centre of prayer only for a season, and the abiding centre is Christ, in whom God has declared himself well pleased, and toward whom the eyes of faith are now to turn as Israel once turned toward the temple. The "day and night" of the verse promises an attention that never sleeps: the eyes that are open upon the place of the name do not close, and the prayer offered toward it, warranted by the word, does not go unregarded.
21:
The petition reaches its refrain — "hear thou from heaven thy dwelling place; and when thou hearest, forgive." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks the word Solomon chooses for entreaty: "supplications" is tahanunim, a term chiefly poetic and late, which never appears in the older account and only here in all of Chronicles, where Kings uses the older synonym tehinnah. And he notes a difference in the direction of the hearing: the chronicler writes "hear thou from thy dwelling place, even from heaven," where the older text at 1 Kings 8:30 has the more unusual and pregnant "unto" — probably the original — as though the prayer rose "unto" heaven and God heard it there.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley fastens on the logic of the request: God is asked to help his people not without their praying but "in answer to their prayers." Even Christ's intercession, Wesley observes, does not supersede but encourage our supplications — the Mediator's prayer above does not excuse us from prayer below but invites it. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry says the same and presses further on the closing word. Solomon asks that God would hear from his true dwelling-place, heaven — for heaven is his dwelling still, not this temple, and thence help must come. And "when thou hearest, forgive": the forgiveness of sins, Henry notes, is that which makes way for all the other answers to our prayers; remove the guilt and you remove the obstruction, so that pardon is the gate through which every other mercy passes.

The witnesses do not divide, and Wesley and Henry stand almost as one. Ellicott secures the letter — the rare word for supplication, the heaven that is God's real dwelling over against the house below — while Wesley and Henry draw the doctrine: that prayer is the appointed channel of God's help, that the intercession of Christ makes prayer more necessary and not less, and that forgiveness is the first and foundational answer without which no other can come. The verse quietly corrects any thought that the temple has localized God: he is asked to hear "from heaven," his dwelling-place, and the house on earth is only the place toward which the prayer is turned, not the place in which God is confined. And the little clause "when thou hearest, forgive" sets the note for all the cases that follow, every one of which will end in the plea for pardon — as though Solomon knew that whatever the calamity, the deepest need of the people would always be the same, and the deepest mercy always forgiveness. Henry's image is exact: pardon is the door, and once it opens, every other blessing may enter.
23:
The case of the oath closes with a plea for righteous judgment — "then hear thou from heaven, and do, and judge thy servants, by requiting the wicked... and by justifying the righteous." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the chronicler writes "from heaven," where the older account at 1 Kings 8:32 has no preposition — perhaps meaning "to heaven" — the chronicler substituting the more ordinary expression, as he does repeatedly through the prayer at vv.25, 30, and following. He weighs the two verbs of judgment. "By requiting the wicked" is, in the older text, "so as to find guilty" — probably the original — and this, he shows, answers exactly to its opposite: "to find guilty a guilty man" balances "justifying a just one," the two clauses set as a perfect antithesis, condemnation of the one matched by acquittal of the other.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers this petition with the like plea at v.30 as a request that God would give judgment according to equity upon all the appeals that should be made to him. And this, Henry says, we may in faith pray for, "for we are sure it shall be done" — God sitteth on the throne judging right. The prayer asks nothing uncertain; it asks God to be what he unchangeably is, the righteous Judge who will not condemn the innocent nor clear the guilty.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott fixes the balanced antithesis of the Hebrew — guilty found guilty, righteous found righteous — and Henry rests the whole petition on the certainty of God's justice. Between them the verse sets forth the temple as the place where the wronged may look for vindication and the false swearer for exposure, both by the hand of a God who cannot be deceived and will not be bribed. There is a note of confidence here that runs deeper than in the other cases, for whereas mercy on the penitent must be sought as grace, righteous judgment may be asked as a thing already sure: to pray that God will condemn the wicked and justify the just is only to ask him to sit upon his throne and be himself. Henry's "we are sure it shall be done" catches the ground of that confidence exactly. The verse thus closes the first case not with anxiety but with rest — the rest of knowing that the appeal made before the altar comes at last to a Judge whose sentence is always according to truth, requiting each man as his cause deserves.
24:
The second case turns from the individual to the nation, and from the courtroom to the battlefield — "if thy people Israel be put to the worse before the enemy, because they have sinned against thee; and shall return and confess thy name, and pray and make supplication before thee in this house." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the chronicler's construction differs from the older "when thy people Israel are smitten" at 1 Kings 8:33; "be put to the worse" is his phrase for defeat. And "because they have sinned" he renders more exactly "when" or "if they sin" — the same conditional that governs the parallel case of drought at v.26 — the Greek and the versions varying between "if" and "when," the older text using a rarer form for the same sense.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the case against the settled tranquillity of Solomon's own reign. The king did not think the mountain of Israel's prosperity stood so strong that it could not be moved; he foresaw that troublesome times would come, and — more searchingly — he expected that sin would move it. The very supposition of defeat is a confession that the nation's peace was held on the tenure of obedience, and that apostasy would forfeit it. Yet Henry marks the mercy folded into the case: those who had not called upon God in their ease would seek him in their affliction; trouble would drive to God even those who had said to him, "Depart."

The witnesses do not divide, and the shape of the case is plain between them. Defeat before the enemy is not treated as mere misfortune but as chastening — "because they have sinned" — and the remedy prescribed is not a better army but a returning people: to turn again, confess the name of God, and pray toward the house. Ellicott's care over the conditional keeps the moral logic exact, for the "if they sin" makes the disaster the consequence of the guilt, and the prayer that follows the appointed way back. Henry's observation gives the case its pastoral edge: Solomon builds into the very dedication of the temple the sober expectation that his people will fall into sin and be humbled for it, and he provides beforehand the road home. The house is dedicated not only for days of triumph but for days of rout, so that a beaten and repentant nation, streaming back to God, may find in the temple a place to confess and be heard. The verse thus makes the sanctuary a refuge for the defeated — not a talisman to prevent defeat, but the appointed door through which a chastened people may return to the God whose hand had smitten them.
27:
The case of drought reaches its plea — "then hear thou from heaven, and forgive the sin of thy servants... when thou hast taught them the good way wherein they should walk; and send rain upon thy land." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the opening "hear thou in heaven," as in the older account, one of the many places where the chronicler has smoothed the preposition. The clause "when thou... the good way" he reads as "for thou pointest them to the good way" — a construction found only here — and, comparing the simpler accusative of Psalms 27:11, "teach me thy way," he judges the older reading at 1 Kings 8:36 probably right, a single letter having been altered; restored, the verse coincides exactly with the parallel.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers this petition with its fellows at vv.25, 38, and 39 as the recurring prayer that God would return in mercy to his people when they repented, reformed, and sought unto him. And this, Henry says, we also may in faith pray for, building upon the repeated declarations God has made of his readiness to accept penitents. The plea rests not on the people's deserving but on the revealed disposition of God to receive those who turn.

The witnesses do not divide, and the case yields a striking order of mercies. God is asked first to forgive the sin, then to teach the good way, and only then to send the withheld rain — as though the drought itself were a schoolmaster, and the true remedy for a parched land were a corrected people. Ellicott's reading, "for thou pointest them to the good way," makes the teaching God's own act: the very affliction is the means by which he instructs, and the rain that follows is at once the relief of the ground and the seal of the lesson learned. Henry's confidence that we may pray for such returning mercy keeps the case from despair; the God who shut up the heavens for sin stands ever ready to open them for penitence. So the verse binds together judgment and instruction and grace in a single movement: the God who sends the drought sends it to teach, forgives the sin that caused it, points the sinner to the good way, and then, the lesson learned and the guilt pardoned, sends the rain upon his land. The physical mercy waits upon the moral one, and the whole discipline ends where all Solomon's cases end — in a forgiven and instructed people, walking the good way to which their God has pointed them.
28:
The prayer widens to take in every calamity that can fall upon a land — "if there be dearth in the land, if there be pestilence, if there be blasting, or mildew, locusts, or caterpillers; if their enemies besiege them in the cities of their land." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the wording of the siege clause: "if their enemies besiege them" is more exactly "if his enemies besiege him," the older account at 1 Kings 8:37 reading "enemy" in the singular, as again at v.34. The phrase "in the cities of their land" he finds difficult — the Hebrew is literally the strange "in the land of his gates" — and, weighing the Greek "before their cities" and the Latin and Syriac renderings, he inclines with Bertheau to read "in the land, at his gates."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets this heaping-up of disasters among Solomon's sober suppositions — that though Israel then enjoyed a profound peace, troublesome times would surely come. The catalogue of famine and plague, blight and locust and siege, is the king's frank acknowledgment that no prosperity is proof against calamity, and that each of these strokes, when it falls, is meant to drive the people to God. Trouble, Henry observes, will bring to their knees even those who in their ease had bidden God depart; and the very variety of the afflictions here foreseen shows how many doors of distress the Lord may open to bring a wandering people home.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott recovers the exact sense of the enemy at the gates, and Henry reads the whole grim inventory as a provision of mercy in disguise. What is most notable in the verse is its sweep: Solomon anticipates not one trial but a whole procession of them — the failure of the harvest, the sickness that empties a city, the blight and mildew that rot the standing grain, the locust swarms that strip the fields bare, the besieging army that pens a people within their walls. Every one of these he brings beforehand under the shadow of the temple, so that whatever the affliction, there is a place to which the sufferer may turn and pray. The house is dedicated not for a world without disaster but for a world full of it; and the long list of calamities, far from darkening the prayer, only enlarges the mercy, since each named plague is matched by the assurance that the God who dwells in the house will hear the cry it wrings from his people. Henry's point stands over the whole: these troubles are foreseen not to frighten but to fold the nation back upon its God, every stroke a summons to the sanctuary.
29:
From the public calamities the prayer descends to the private wound — "what prayer or what supplication soever shall be made of any man, or of all thy people Israel, when every one shall know his own sore and his own grief, and shall spread forth his hands in this house." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the opening "when" or "if," the same Hebrew relative that governs the earlier cases at v.24. His chief note falls on the phrase "his own sore and his own grief," which the chronicler gives where the older account at 1 Kings 8:38 has "the plague of his own heart"; the chronicler's expression, Ellicott suggests, reads almost like a gloss upon that older and more inward phrase, spelling out as "sore" and "grief" what Kings had located in the heart.

The single witness leaves the verse to its own tenderness, and the tenderness is considerable. After the sweeping list of national disasters — famine, plague, blight, siege — the prayer suddenly narrows to the individual, to the one man who "knows his own sore and his own grief." The affliction here is not the public catastrophe but the private ache, the trouble each heart carries and each heart alone fully knows; and it is precisely this that Solomon brings within the reach of the temple. The great house built for a nation is opened to the single sufferer, so that the man with a grief he can scarcely name to his neighbour may yet spread forth his hands in this house and be heard.

There is a deliberate movement in the placing of the verse, from the many to the one, from the plague that empties a city to the plague of a single heart. Ellicott's observation that the chronicler seems to unfold the older "plague of his own heart" into "his own sore and his own grief" only underlines the inwardness of it: whatever the outward occasion, the real burden is felt within, and it is the inward burden that drives a man to prayer. The verse thus grants the temple a reach that no merely national institution could have — it becomes the resort not only of armies and assemblies but of the solitary and the sorrowful, of every man who, knowing the particular wound that no one else can measure, lifts his hands toward the place where God has set his name. Solomon foresees that true prayer is finally personal, wrung from a self-knowledge of one's own grief; and he asks that God would meet each such prayer, so that the house dedicated for a whole people should also be a house for the lonely heart that knows its own sore and brings it, and it alone, to God.
30:
The plea over the private grief rises to the God who reads the heart — "then hear thou from heaven thy dwelling place, and forgive, and render unto every man according unto all his ways, whose heart thou knowest; (for thou only knowest the hearts of the children of men)." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders "every man" more exactly as "the man," a distributive use of the article that individualizes the judgment — each man dealt with singly. "Whose heart thou knowest" he reads "because thou knowest his heart," making God's knowledge the very ground of the just recompense; and he notes that in the closing clause the word "all" has dropped from the received text — "the hearts of all the children of men" — though it is preserved in some manuscripts, the versions, and the older account at 1 Kings 8:39.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry seizes the parenthesis as one of the prayer's great doctrinal pillars: that God, and he only, knows the hearts of the children of men. All men's thoughts, aims, and affections, Henry says, are naked and open before him; however the imaginations and intents of our hearts may be concealed from men, from angels, and even from devils, they cannot be hidden from God, who knows not only what is in the heart but the heart itself and all the beatings of it. Here is the truth that makes the temple more than a place of outward rite — the God who dwells there searches the inward man.

The witnesses do not divide, and the two labours meet in a single confession. Ellicott fixes the individualizing grammar — "the man," judged singly, "because thou knowest his heart" — and Henry unfolds the doctrine of God's sole omniscience over the human heart. The connection with the verse before is exact: there the sufferer alone "knew his own sore and his own grief," his trouble hidden in his own breast; here God is confessed to know that hidden thing better than the man himself. What no neighbour can measure and no court can reach, God sees to the bottom; and it is on this that the justice of his rendering rests, for he can give to every man "according to all his ways" only because he reads the heart from which the ways proceed. The doctrine cuts both ways, as comfort and as awe: the penitent may be sure that the grief he cannot express is fully known, and the hypocrite may be sure that the guilt he conceals is fully seen. Henry's phrase — that God knows "all the beatings" of the heart — leaves the worshipper before a God from whom nothing is hidden, whose forgiveness and whose recompense are alike dispensed by one who needs no witnesses, because he alone knows what is in man.
31:
The petition names its end — "that they may fear thee, to walk in thy ways, so long as they live in the land which thou gavest unto our fathers." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott's note is a single stroke: the clause "to walk in thy ways" is an explanatory remark added by the chronicler, absent from the older account, spelling out what the fear of God was meant to produce. The small addition is worth its weight, for it fixes the aim of the whole preceding plea.

What the verse establishes is the purpose that answered prayer is meant to serve. Solomon has asked that God would hear, forgive, and render to every man according to his heart; and now he says why — "that they may fear thee." The end of all this mercy is not merely the relief of the sufferer but the reverence of the people; God forgives in order that he may be feared, and hears in order that his ways may be walked in. The chronicler's own added words, "to walk in thy ways," make the fear practical: it is no mere emotion of dread but a settled reverence that shows itself in obedience, in the daily walking of the paths God has marked out.

Ellicott's spare comment leaves the theology of the purpose-clause to declare itself, and it is a theology the whole prayer assumes. Every mercy Solomon asks — pardon after defeat, rain after drought, hearing in the day of private grief — is asked with a view to this: that the people, having tasted the goodness of God, should fear and obey him all their days in the land he gave their fathers. The experience of forgiveness is meant to breed not presumption but reverence; the God who answers prayer thereby binds the answered to himself. And the added phrase ties the fear to the tenure of the land — "so long as they live in the land which thou gavest unto our fathers" — so that reverence and obedience are the very condition on which the gift of the land is enjoyed, echoing the promise of v.16 that the throne itself stands upon obedience. The chronicler, adding "to walk in thy ways," will not let the fear of God float free of conduct: it must come down into the streets and fields of ordinary life, or it is not the fear he means. So the verse turns the whole apparatus of prayer and pardon toward its true goal — a people who, because their God hears and forgives, revere him and walk in his ways as long as they live.
32:
The prayer breaks its national bounds to make room for the outsider — "moreover concerning the stranger, which is not of thy people Israel, but is come from a far country for thy great name's sake, and thy mighty hand, and thy stretched out arm; if they come and pray in this house." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that in this verse the chronicler has run together what the older account gives as two verses, 1 Kings 8:41-42, probably by an error of transcription that compressed them; the fuller original tells how the stranger, hearing of God's "great name and mighty hand and stretched-out arm," would come and pray toward the house. "But is come" he renders "and shall come," a future expectation rather than a past fact.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry marks this as one of Solomon's reasonable suppositions — that strangers would come from afar to worship the God of Israel and pay him homage. And this, Henry says, might well be expected, considering what worthless things the gods of the nations were, and what proofs the God of Israel had already given of his being Lord of the whole earth. The very emptiness of the heathen idols, set against the mighty acts of the LORD, would draw thoughtful men of every people toward Jerusalem.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse opens a window that the rest of the prayer had kept shut. Every earlier case had concerned Israel — Israel defeated, Israel under drought, Israel besieged, the individual Israelite with his private grief. Now Solomon deliberately turns to "the stranger, which is not of thy people Israel," and asks that the foreigner drawn by report of God's great name should not be turned away. What draws the stranger is precisely what draws Israel — the fame of God's "great name, mighty hand, and stretched-out arm," the language of the Exodus now sounding in far countries. Ellicott's care to restore the fuller original keeps that triad intact, the very words by which God had made himself known in delivering his people; and Henry's observation supplies the motive, that the manifest superiority of the living God over the worthless idols of the nations would fetch worshippers from the ends of the earth. The temple, in Solomon's vision, is not a private chapel for one nation but a house whose God is Lord of all, and whose name, going out into all lands, will bring the distant Gentile to pray where Israel prays. The verse plants, at the heart of the dedication, the seed of a hope far larger than Israel — that the God of Jacob should be sought and found by the stranger from a far country.
33:
The stranger's prayer is answered with the boldest petition in the whole dedication — "then hear thou from heaven, even from thy dwelling place, and do according to all that the stranger calleth to thee for; that all people of the earth may know thy name, and fear thee, as doth thy people Israel." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the chronicler opens with the connective "and," which the older account at 1 Kings 8:43 omits but which agrees with the particle used in the earlier cases. "People" he corrects to "the peoples" — the nations of the earth in their plurality — and the closing "and fear thee" he would render without the conjunction, "that they may fear thee," as in the older text.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads it as Solomon's prayer that God would bid the strangers welcome to this house and answer their prayers; for, he asks, if there be one duty required of the stranger and the native alike, why should there not be one privilege granted to both?
