He appeals to the law's own equity, "one law for the stranger and for one born in the land," at Leviticus 24:22. The foreigner who prays is to be heard as freely as the Israelite.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse reaches a height the prayer had been climbing toward. Solomon asks God to grant the stranger not merely a hearing but the very substance of his request — "all that the stranger calleth to thee for" — a fuller assurance, it has often been observed, than he asks even for Israel, whose prayers are answered with pardon and discipline; the outsider is to receive all he asks. And the reason is missionary: that all the peoples of the earth may come to know the name of God and to fear him as Israel does. The particular house in Jerusalem is thus made the instrument of a universal design — the place where a stranger's answered prayer becomes a testimony to his whole nation, spreading the knowledge and fear of the true God to the ends of the earth. Ellicott's "the peoples" keeps the horizon as wide as the world; Henry's "one law, one privilege" keeps the door as open to the Gentile as to the Jew. The verse anticipates, within the walls of the old covenant, the reach of the new — a God whose house shall be a house of prayer for all peoples, and whose name, made known through answered prayer, shall be feared far beyond the borders of Israel.
34:
The prayer returns to Israel at war, and to the direction in which a soldier turns his face — "if thy people go out to war against their enemies by the way that thou shalt send them, and they pray unto thee toward this city which thou hast chosen, and the house which I have built for thy name." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fixes the idiom: "toward this city" is literally "the way of this city" — that is, in the direction of it — the same construction he marks again at v.38, where the exiles pray "the way of their land." The phrase describes not a place of prayer but a bearing, the line along which the prayer is aimed.

The single witness leaves the theology of directed prayer to speak, and it is the same principle the prayer has touched before at v.20. The armies of Israel, marching out "by the way that thou shalt send them" — on wars undertaken at God's bidding, not their own — are to pray in the field with their faces turned toward the chosen city and the house built for the name. Distance does not sever them from the sanctuary; wherever the campaign carries them, they orient their prayer toward Jerusalem, as toward the fixed point where God has set his name and promised to look. The soldier far from the temple prays no less to the temple's God, and the turning of his face toward the city is the outward sign of a heart that seeks help from the God who dwells there.

There is a quiet discipline in the clause "by the way that thou shalt send them," and Ellicott's care over the idiom of direction only sharpens it. The prayer of the army is heard on the assumption that the war is God's — that they go out where he sends them — so that their turning toward the city in prayer matches their going out at his command. Both are acts of dependence: they march by his direction and they pray toward his house, and the two together confess that neither the going nor the fighting is their own. The verse thus makes even the battlefield an extension of the temple courts; the God who cannot be contained in the house is yet prayed to "toward" the house from the farthest frontier, and the whole land, and the armies beyond its borders, are gathered by this orientation of prayer around the single place where the name of God dwells. Ellicott's "the way of this city" restores the vivid picture — a host in the field, faces set toward Jerusalem, praying along the line that runs back to the house of God — and lets the geography of the prayer preach its own quiet lesson, that no distance puts a praying servant out of reach of the God whose name is on the house.
35:
The prayer for the army at war ends where the others end, in an appeal for God to take up his people's side — "then hear thou from heaven their prayer and their supplication, and maintain their cause." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders "maintain their cause" more exactly as "do," or "accomplish, their right" — the Vulgate boldly turns it "avenge them" — a plea that God would see justice done to Israel and give effect to their rightful claim against the enemy.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers this petition with the like plea at v.39 as a request that God would, upon all occasions, own and plead the cause of his people against all the opposers of it. And Henry states the ground of it in a sentence: "if they be the Israel of God, their cause is the cause of God, and he would espouse it." The people's quarrel, when the people are God's, is not theirs only but his; and so they may ask him not merely to hear their prayer but to make their right prevail.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott fixes the force of the plea — that God would accomplish Israel's right, do them justice, avenge their wrong — and Henry supplies the theology that makes such a plea legitimate: the identification of the people's cause with God's own. This is why the prayer of the army may rise beyond a request for safety to a request for vindication. The soldiers do not merely ask to be spared; they ask that their cause be maintained, that the God whose name they bear should see their right upheld against those who oppose them. The confidence rests entirely on the "if" that Henry supplies — if they are indeed the Israel of God, walking in his ways and warring at his sending, then their cause and his are one, and he will not leave it undefended. The verse thus completes the case of the war: the God who sent them out and toward whose house they prayed is asked, from heaven, to prosecute their right as his own. And it points, as Henry's phrase quietly does, beyond the battles of old Israel to every people that is truly God's, whose cause, being his, he may be trusted to espouse — hearing the prayer, and doing the right, of those who are his own. The plea is modest in its words and vast in its reach: to ask God to maintain a cause is to ask him to make himself party to the quarrel, and to stake his own honour on the outcome of his people's war.
38:
The confession becomes a whole-hearted turning — "if they return to thee with all their heart and with all their soul in the land of their captivity, whither they have carried them captives, and pray toward their land, which thou gavest unto their fathers, and toward the city which thou hast chosen, and toward the house which I have built for thy name." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott weighs the wording of the exile's plight. The chronicler writes "in the land of their captivity, whither they have carried them captives," where the older account at 1 Kings 8:48 has "in the land of their enemies who carried them captives"; the Syriac reads "in the cities of their captors." Perhaps, Ellicott suggests, "their captivity" is a corruption of "their captors," or else the relative refers to the land itself, "which carried them captive."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers this petition with its fellows at v.39 as the recurring prayer that God would return in mercy to his people when they repented, reformed, and sought unto him — a mercy, Henry has insisted, that we may in faith pray for, resting on God's repeated declarations of his readiness to accept penitents. The exile who turns with all his heart and soul is turning toward a God who has promised to receive him.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse gathers up the whole prayer's teaching on directed prayer and fixes it at the farthest possible distance. Earlier the soldier at war was to pray "toward this city"; now the captive in a foreign land, hundreds of miles from Zion, is to pray "toward their land, and the city, and the house" — the three concentric circles of the promise, the gift of the land, the choice of the city, the building of the house, all held in the exile's mind as he turns his face homeward in prayer. The distance only makes the orientation more poignant: stripped of the land, shut out of the city, barred from the house, the penitent has nothing left but the direction of his longing, and he prays along the line that runs back to the place where God set his name. Ellicott's care over the wording of the captivity keeps the setting vivid — a people in the land of those who carried them away — and Henry's assurance keeps the hope alive, that such a turning, with all the heart and all the soul, meets a God ready to accept it. The verse asks nothing of the exiles' circumstances, which are hopeless, and everything of their hearts, which may yet be whole toward God; and it makes the distant, faced temple the pledge that a wholehearted return will not be turned away.
39:
The last petition of the great series rises to its threefold plea — "then hear thou from the heavens, even from thy dwelling place, their prayer and their supplications, and maintain their cause, and forgive thy people which have sinned against thee." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that "their supplications" stands in the plural where the older account at 1 Kings 8:49 has the singular, a plural found nowhere else and probably not original here. And he marks a significant seam: the clause "and forgive thy people" is the first clause of 1 Kings 8:50, and from this point to the end of Solomon's prayer the two texts run wholly apart — the chronicler, having followed Kings through all the cases, now turning to a close of his own drawn from elsewhere.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads this verse with its companion at v.35 as the prayer that God would own and plead his people's cause, and with the earlier pleas as the prayer that he would return in mercy to the penitent. Here the two strands meet: God is asked both to "maintain their cause" and to "forgive," to vindicate them against their enemies and to pardon their sin against himself. For if they be the Israel of God, their cause is the cause of God; and yet, being sinners, their first need from God is forgiveness.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse makes a fitting climax to the whole succession of cases. Every petition since the courtroom of v.22 has ended in a plea for God to hear from heaven; and now, at the deepest point of the people's need — captive, penitent, praying toward a ruined house from a far land — the plea gathers all its themes into three words: hear, maintain, forgive. Ellicott's note that Kings and Chronicles here part company marks the moment as a hinge; the borrowed cases are done, and the chronicler is about to add his own conclusion. But before he does, the prayer states its heart. What Israel finally needs is not merely to be heard, nor merely to have their cause upheld against the nations, but to be forgiven the sin that brought the whole disaster upon them. Henry's linking of vindication and pardon catches the double note exactly: God must be both the advocate of his people's right and the pardoner of their wrong. And that "forgive thy people which have sinned against thee" is where the long list of cases has been tending all along — for whatever the calamity, defeat or drought or plague or exile, the root was sin, and the only sufficient mercy is that God should forgive it. The verse closes the borrowed pattern on the note that all Solomon's prayer has sounded: pardon, from heaven, for a people who have nothing to plead but the mercy of their God.
40:
The prayer draws to its close with a last summarizing appeal — "Now, my God, let, I beseech thee, thine eyes be open, and let thine ears be attent unto the prayer that is made in this place." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott links the plea for open eyes back to the petition at v.20 and forward to the divine answer at 2 Chronicles 7:15, where the whole verse is taken up again and returned to Solomon as a promise. "Attent," he explains, means "attentive," or "listening" — a word, qashshuboth, so rare that it occurs elsewhere only in the late Psalms 130:2, "let thine ears be attentive to the voice of my supplications." And the phrase "the prayer that is made in this place" is itself unusual, found only here and in the answering promise of the next chapter.

The single witness leaves the verse to gather the whole prayer into its two organs of attention — the eyes and the ears of God. Solomon has asked, again and again, that God would "hear from heaven"; now he asks it once more, but in the tenderest and most personal form, opening with "Now, my God" — no longer "O LORD God of Israel" but "my God," the possessive of a heart that has poured itself out and now rests its whole case on the nearness of the God it addresses. The petition is stripped to essentials: eyes open, ears attentive, to the prayer of this place. All the particular cases are behind him; what remains is the simple, comprehensive request that God would watch and listen.

Ellicott's cross-reference to the next chapter gives the verse its quiet triumph. What Solomon here asks — open eyes, attentive ears — God will presently grant in almost the same words, appearing to the king by night to say, "Now mine eyes shall be open, and mine ears attent unto the prayer that is made in this place." The petition is thus a prayer that is about to be answered in its own language, the request and the reply matched phrase for phrase. And the rare word "attent," borrowed from the same root as the psalmist's cry from the depths, colours the whole with the note of earnest listening — a God who does not merely hear but bends to attend, whose ears are inclined toward the place where his people pray. The verse leaves the temple established as exactly what Solomon asked it to be: not a house that contains God, but a place upon which the eyes of God are open and toward which his ears are attentive, day and night, to every prayer that is made there. On that assurance the great intercession all but closes, needing only the two verses of benediction that follow to bring it home.
